reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to remediate the audit rule. The previous attempts already did systemctl reload auditd but scan fails. We need a different approach, cannot use systemctl reload auditd. We can still use systemctl restart auditd? But that's similar. Perhaps use auditctl command to add rule temporarily? But policy likely requires file configuration. We need to create audit rules file and ensure auditd picks up. The scanning rule checks that file exists. We need to modify /etc/audit/audit.rules or /etc/audit/rules.d/creat.rules depending on configuration. The system may use augenrules (default), but to be safe we should append to /etc/audit/audit.rules if use_augenrules is not set. Also need to avoid duplicate. We can perform first reading file content. Use read_file to inspect /etc/audit/auditd.conf for use_augenrules line. If it says 'use_augenrules = yes', then use .rules file. If no or disabled, use /etc/audit/audit.rules.

We should read /etc/audit/auditd.conf, check for pattern /use_augenrules\s*=\s*yes/. If matches, we will add to /etc/audit/rules.d/creat.rules. Else we will add to /etc/audit/audit.rules.

We also need to check architecture (32 or 64). We'll add both rules accordingly. But to simplify we can add both arch rules lines. But need to support 64-bit env. We can detect x86_64/arm64 etc. Use $(getconf LONG_BIT) maybe. But within minimal script we could always add both archs.

We just need to create lines if not present.

We need to avoid duplicate. We'll read file content, and check if each line exists with grep -Fx. For each line, if not found, append. We'll do this with sed or echo >>.

Can't chain commands, need to do each file modification one command. We might need multiple commands to append lines. But we can use one command per line append. Tools allow multiple calls.

We must also do systemctl reload? Not allowed. Path: we cannot issue that command. So maybe we just rely on auditctl to reload rules after writing? We can use auditctl -a ... to add; but then also file will contain duplicate older rules. But scanning maybe checks file content only. So we can skip reload command. We can just modify file and rely on auditd service restart? But can't use systemctl. But maybe auditd reloads automatically on configuration file modification? Might not.

Given requirement to not use reload, we could use 'systemctl restart auditd' instead. That is different command, not reload. Allowed? It says "Do NOT repeat any previously-tried commands: AVOID: systemctl reload auditd". So can use systemctl restart? Possibly. But better not rely on restarting causing downtime? But may be allowed. We'll proceed.

So plan: 1) Read /etc/audit/auditd.conf. 2) Determine use_augenrules. 3) Determine target file. 4) For each rule line, read target file to check existence. 5) Append missing lines. 6) Restart auditd.

Ok.

We have to produce plan description. Then review_plan. Let's do that.
